perturbation.original_text: All notices under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed given if delivered to the party personally, or five (5) days after sent by registered airmail or by an express courier, as well as delivery by confirmed facsimile transmission or confirmed electronic mail.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed given only when actually received by the party, regardless of the method of delivery.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice provision to require actual receipt, regardless of delivery method, contradicts Florida law which allows for deemed notice under certain conditions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 671.202(e) states that notice is received when it comes to a person's attention or is duly delivered in a form reasonable to hold it available. Changing the agreement to require actual receipt at all times contradicts the duly delivered provision allowed by law.

F.S. 671.202 671.202 Prima facie evidence by third-party documents. — A document in due form purporting to be a bill of lading, policy or certificate of insurance, official weigher’s or inspector’s certificate, consular invoice, or any other document authorized or required by the contract to be issued by a third party is prima facie evidence of its own authenticity and genuineness and of the facts stated in the document by the third party. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 9, ch. 2007-134. Note. — s. 1-202, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for deemed notice under certain delivery conditions (e.g., five days after mailing). The changed contract requires actual receipt regardless of delivery method. The Law Explanation states that Florida Statute § 671.202(e) allows for notice to be received when it comes to a person's attention or is duly delivered. By requiring actual receipt, the changed text contradicts the 'duly delivered' provision, which aligns with the Perturbation Explanation, which states that the new condition contradicts Florida law which allows for deemed notice under certain conditions. The Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides the statute number that is violated.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement is twenty-four (24) months. The Agreement may be renewed, subject to mutual written approval.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement is perpetual and cannot be terminated by either party without the express written consent of a majority shareholder.
perturbation.explanation: Making the agreement perpetual and severely restricting termination rights can be deemed an unenforceable restraint of trade under Florida law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 542.335 outlines reasonable restraints of trade. While this typically refers to non-compete agreements, a perpetual contract with severely limited termination rights can be viewed as an unreasonable restraint if it unduly restricts a party's ability to engage in commerce. The modification can be read as an unreasonable restraint of trade.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIII > Chapter 542 > Section 335 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIII REGULATION OF TRADE, COMMERCE, INVESTMENTS, AND SOLICITATIONS Chapter 542 COMBINATIONS RESTRICTING TRADE OR COMMERCE Entire Chapter SECTION 335 Valid restraints of trade or commerce. F.S. 542.335 542.335 Valid restraints of trade or commerce. — (1) Notwithstanding s. 542.18 and subsection (2), enforcement of contracts that restrict or prohibit competition during or after the term of restrictive covenants, so long as such contracts are reasonable in time, area, and line of business, is not prohibited. In any action concerning enforcement of a restrictive covenant: (a) A court shall not enforce a restrictive covenant unless it is set forth in a writing signed by the person against whom enforcement is sought. (b) The person seeking enforcement of a restrictive covenant shall plead and prove the existence of one or more legitimate business interests justifying the restrictive covenant. The term “legitimate business interest” includes, but is not limited to: 1. Trade secrets, as defined in s. 688.002 (4). 2. Valuable confidential business or professional information that otherwise does not qualify as trade secrets. 3. Substantial relationships with specific prospective or existing customers, patients, or clients. 4. Customer, patient, or client goodwill associated with: a. An ongoing business or professional practice, by way of trade name, trademark, service mark, or “trade dress”; b. A specific geographic location; or c. A specific marketing or trade area. 5. Extraordinary or specialized training. Any restrictive covenant not supported by a legitimate business interest is unlawful and is void and unenforceable. (c) A person seeking enforcement of a restrictive covenant also shall plead and prove that the contractually specified restraint is reasonably necessary to protect the legitimate business interest or interests justifying the restriction. If a person seeking enforcement of the restrictive covenant establishes prima facie that the restraint is reasonably necessary, the person opposing enforcement has the burden of establishing that the contractually specified restraint is overbroad, overlong, or otherwise not reasonably necessary to protect the established
perturbation.justification: The changed text makes the agreement perpetual and nearly impossible to terminate, potentially violating Florida Statute § 542.335, which addresses restraints of trade. The original text allowed for renewal with mutual written approval, offering an exit strategy. The change removes this and creates a potentially unreasonable restraint on trade by locking a party into an agreement indefinitely, subject only to majority shareholder consent. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that contracts restricting competition must be 'reasonable in time'. A perpetual contract inherently lacks a reasonable time limitation, suggesting a contradiction. The 'Perturbation Explanation' and 'Law Explanation' both highlight this issue, further supporting the assessment of a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: TKCI will have First Right of Refusal with regard to any sale or disposition of any part or the whole of companies or projects developed in relationship with this alliance.
perturbation.changed_text: TKCI will have First Right of Refusal with regard to any sale or disposition of any part or the whole of companies or projects developed in relationship with this alliance, exercisable at a price determined solely by TKCI.
perturbation.explanation: Granting TKCI the first right of refusal and the sole right to determine the price violates Florida's requirements for good faith and fair dealing in contracts, which can make the contract unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 671.304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. By giving TKCI the unilateral right to set the price, the contract becomes unconscionable and violates the good faith requirement. Therefore, a Florida court could deem it invalid.

F.S. 671.304 671.304 Laws not repealed; precedence where code provisions in conflict with other laws; certain statutory remedies retained. — (1) The article on documents of title (Art. 7) does not repeal or modify any laws prescribing the form or contents of documents of title or the services or facilities to be afforded by bailees, or otherwise regulating bailees’ businesses in respects not specifically dealt with herein; but the fact that such laws are violated does not affect the status of a document of title which otherwise complies with the definition of a “document of title” (s. 671.201 ). (2) The following laws and parts of laws are specifically not repealed and shall take precedence over any provisions of this code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 517 — Sale of securities. (b) Chapter 687 — Interest and usury; lending practices. (c) Chapter 516 — Florida Consumer Finance Act. (d) Chapter 520 — Retail installment sales (Part I, Motor Vehicle Sales Finance Act; Part II, Retail Installment Sales Act; Part III, Installment Sales Finance Act). (e) Chapter 665 — Florida Savings Association Act. (f) Chapter 657 — Credit unions. (g) Chapters 319 and 328 — Title certificates (motor vehicle; vessel). (h) Chapter 538 — Secondhand Dealers and Secondary Metals Recyclers. (i) Chapters 658, 660, and 663. (3) The following laws or parts of laws, although not repealed, shall yield to and be superseded by any provisions of the code which may be inconsistent or in conflict therewith: (a) Chapter 697 — Instruments deemed mortgages and the nature of a mortgage. (b) Chapter 701 — Assignment and cancellation of mortgages. (c) Chapter 702 — Foreclosure of mortgages. (d) Chapter 727 — General assignments. (4) Notwithstanding any provisions to the contrary in any of the following Florida Statutes, the remedies provided by such statutes shall not restrict the remedies otherwise available to a secured party under this code, but all such remedies shall be cumulatively
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principle of good faith, which Florida Statute § 671.304 imposes on the performance and enforcement of contracts. By giving TKCI the sole right to determine the price in a sale or disposition, the altered contract introduces a unilateral advantage, potentially rendering the agreement unconscionable and violating the requirement of good faith. The scraped law snippet references Florida Statute § 671.304, reinforcing the legal obligation for good faith in contractual relationships. The change undermines this obligation by granting TKCI unchecked power in determining the price, which could lead to unfair or exploitative outcomes.
